what_it_does: A static HTML/CSS desktop mockup for the RHM 'twin' surface, visualizing the boundary between verified functionality (model_of_tim grounding, provenance grading, echo-guard) and proposed functionality (predicting Tim's judgement, corpus ingestion).
observations: The file opens with a long honesty contract that splits every feature into EXISTS or PROPOSED.
The UI has a top bar, left node-palette rail, canvas, activity footer, and a right-hand 'Twin' panel tab.
The canvas shows an isolated model_of_tim content node marked VOLATILE, fail-loud, and status RAN.
The right panel shows provenance grades (gold / working) attached to every turn, with operator vs twin source.
An echo-guard section visualizes a twin output resubmitted as a 'user' turn being rejected from the training signal.
A proposed section shows cold-start abstention as a first-class state and deliberately omits any confidence score.
data-ui-ref attributes are scattered throughout, including ui://twin and run://system/model_of_tim.
A mobile tabbar is included but hidden on desktop.
standouts: The EXISTS/PROPOSED split is made structural in the chrome and repeated on every section header.
The echo-guard is rendered as an explicit accept/reject lane diagram.
The model_of_tim node is shown as a real, volatile, fail-loud content node on the canvas.
Cold-start abstention is presented as an honest, visible state rather than a missing or hidden feature.
conventions: Uses the shared design-system.css variables, Bricolage Grotesque headings, and IBM Plex Mono for code-like labels.
Follows the mockup pattern of a grid-based app shell with top bar, rail, canvas, footer, and side panel.
Uses data-ui-ref attributes to tag elements with conceptual addresses.
Breaks pure mockup neutrality by including a prescriptive address-registration note and a suggested address in the header.
concerns: The header comment states addresses.json has NO dedicated twin address, while the panel footnote claims ui://twin is already registered.
The mockup is entirely presentational HTML/CSS with no interactivity or behavior defined.
The heavy inline CSS and many data-ui-ref attributes make the file brittle and hard to maintain.
The proposed section is visually prominent and could be mistaken for shipped functionality at a glance.
notes: A design mockup that doubles as a manifesto for honest feature-boundary labeling, showing what the twin actually does today versus what is still proposed.
questions: Is ui://twin already registered in addresses.json or is it only a suggested registration?
What triggers the provenance grade labels and are they computed or manually annotated in this view?
How does the model_of_tim node connect to the rest of the graph beyond the single output port shown?
What exactly is the D8 corpus ingestion dependency and where is it documented?
Does the presence dial or any tab have defined interaction behavior, or is it purely visual?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is a desktop mockup of the twin/grading surface, and the file matches that; it also acts as a design contract by embedding the EXISTS/PROPOSED split.
apparent_themes: Radical honesty about built versus unbuilt capabilities
No confidence scores — posture over numbers
intention_signals: The file is trying to establish a visual language where the twin panel cannot be read as more capable than the code actually is, while still making the proposed future state legible.
novelty: It treats abstention and the EXISTS/PROPOSED boundary as first-class UI chrome rather than footnotes, and it renders the echo-guard as a visible accept/reject pipeline.
contribution: A concrete pattern for separating verified substrate from proposed features in a twin panel, plus reusable visuals for provenance grades, echo-guard verdicts, volatile content nodes, and cold-start abstention.
summary_for_embedding: An HTML/CSS desktop mockup of the RHM twin panel that visualizes provenance grading, echo-guarding, the volatile model_of_tim content node, and a clearly labeled proposed cold-start prediction feature.
intention_for_embedding: To design a twin surface that honestly distinguishes verified code behavior from proposed future behavior and prevents users from mistaking abstention or unbuilt prediction for a confidence score.
